sitting_overview: The Commons sitting on 2 June 2026 was dominated by a statement on the murder of 18-year-old Henry Nowak, in which the Home Secretary committed to a fully resourced Independent Office for Police Conduct investigation, rejected calls to review the kirpan religious exemption, and refused to frame the case as one of preferential treatment of communities. An urgent question on the Milburn interim report on young people not in education, employment or training, departmental questions to the Energy Secretary set against the war in Iran and a 13 per cent price cap rise, the Committee stage of the Armed Forces Bill on housing, justice and reserves, and an adjournment debate on systemic Department for Work and Pensions errors affecting Scottish carers filled the rest of the day.

highlights.summary: The Home Secretary, Shabana Mahmood, said the Independent Office for Police Conduct will be resourced to investigate the police response to Henry Nowak's death, ruled out collective blame on the Sikh community, and set the family's call against further division as the test for the wider response.
highlights.key_quote: We do not believe in collective punishment in this country. Instead, we stand together against an act of pure evil.
highlights.analysis: Mahmood spoke the day after Vickrum Digwa was sentenced to life with a minimum 21-year term, with further charges authorised against members of his family and Digwa's mother convicted of assisting an offender. She paired the IOPC commitment with the Government's halving knife crime plan, citing a 10 per cent fall in knife crime and a 27 per cent fall in knife homicides to a decade low, and named misinformation online and threats against police officers as a danger that the House should jointly condemn. She also flagged that the Offensive Weapons Act 2019 protections for the kirpan are not being reopened.

debate_map.quote: We have a knife problem in this country. That is why we have taken early steps to ban zombie knives and crack down on knife sellers, but it is obvious that that is not enough and we have to go further.

many white people in this country feel that the police are so terrified of being called racist that they either underreact, as in the case of the appalling grooming scandal, or they overreact, as in this case
I pay tribute to my constituent, Henry Nowak, a bright young man at the start of a life that held so much promise.

highlights.summary: The shadow Home Secretary, Chris Philp (CON), pressed the Home Secretary to withdraw the College of Policing's March 2025 'police anti-racism commitment', saying it directs forces to equalise arrest rates between ethnic groups despite differing offending rates.
highlights.key_quote: The police anti-racism commitment, published in March 2025 by the National Police Chiefs’ Council and the College of Policing, urges police forces to reverse-engineer arrest rates for ethnic groups so that those rates are the same, even though the offending rates are different, by treating different ethnic groups differently.
highlights.analysis: Philp linked the police response in the Nowak case to the College of Policing document, and to the Valdo Calocane case in Nottingham and the Axel Rudakubana case in Southport, where the Opposition argues that race-based assumptions affected risk assessments. The Home Secretary did not agree to withdraw the document, noting that the race action plan for the police began under the previous Conservative Government and that policing by consent requires confidence from all communities. Sir Julian Lewis (CON) put the same withdrawal request to her later in the statement.

debate_map.quote: the race action plan for the police began life under the previous Conservative Government—in fact, I am old enough to remember when Theresa May called out the disproportionate use of stop and search in black communities in particular.
will she undertake to examine and withdraw the policing policy document identified by the shadow Home Secretary as embodying precisely such differential treatment?

highlights.summary: Robert Jenrick (REF) asked the Home Secretary to affirm that 'white lives matter just as much as anyone else's' on public safety, attributing the police failures in the Nowak case and earlier killings to what he called the 'anti-racism agenda'.
highlights.key_quote: Will she say that when it comes to public safety, white lives matter just as much as anyone else’s?
highlights.analysis: Jenrick named Valdo Calocane and Axel Rudakubana alongside Digwa, casting their cases as evidence of a 'sickness' rooted in anti-racism policy. The Home Secretary refused the framing, saying it was a moment to reflect on a tragedy rather than to pit white Britons against non-white Britons, and that the IOPC must establish the facts of officer conduct before lessons are drawn. Suella Braverman (REF) put the same line about false allegations of racism to her later in the statement, and was rebuffed in the same terms.

debate_map.quote: I do not think this is a moment to pit white Britons against non-white Britons; this is a moment to reflect on a horrific tragedy.
Does the Home Secretary agree that a false accusation of racism against a white person is just as bad as, if not worse than, a legitimate claim of racism, because of the deceit?

Great leadership has already been shown by the family of Henry Nowak in the tenor of their response and their statement, and in their desire—rightly, in my view—not to see this murder used as a tool for furthering division in our country.

highlights.summary: The Parliamentary Under-Secretary, Andrew Western, told the Commons that £2.5 billion in youth-guarantee funding will deliver half a million opportunities for young people to work, train or take apprenticeships, in response to Alan Milburn's interim report on those not in education, employment or training.
highlights.key_quote: During their last few years in power, the number of young people not in education, employment or training rose by a quarter of a million—a shameful legacy.
highlights.analysis: Western said the report shows the problem is 'deep-seated and long-term' rather than a feature of the last year or two, and read paragraph 266 of the report into the record to rebut Conservative claims that recent national insurance changes had driven the rise. He set out the Government's response: a youth guarantee with referral to one of six options at three months, a £3,000 grant for small and medium-sized enterprises hiring apprentices, and a £2,000 grant for employers hiring a young person who has been on universal credit for six months or more. The full Milburn recommendations are expected later in the year.

debate_map.quote: Last week, the Secretary of State was only too eager to talk about this report on the telly. Where is he today?
I really think that the shadow Secretary of State, the hon. Member for Faversham and Mid Kent (Helen Whately), should recognise that and apologise.

highlights.summary: The shadow Energy Secretary, Claire Coutinho (CON), put a yes-or-no question to the Energy Secretary asking him to guarantee that no solar panel installed on a British primary school under his Government's schemes had been produced by Chinese forced labour, a guarantee the Secretary of State did not give.
highlights.key_quote: I have a yes-or-no question for the Secretary of State: can he guarantee that not a single solar panel put on a British primary school by his Government has been produced by Chinese slave labour?
highlights.analysis: Coutinho's question followed an earlier oral question by Bradley Thomas (CON) citing that 45 per cent of the world's solar-grade polysilicon comes from the Xinjiang Uyghur region and a Politico investigation finding that five of seven Great British Energy school contracts could not guarantee freedom from forced labour. The Energy Secretary, Ed Miliband, said the regime has been strengthened through the Great British Energy Bill and that Frances O'Grady is now the champion on slave labour, but did not give the guarantee Coutinho asked for.

debate_map.quote: I can absolutely assure the shadow Secretary of State that we will do everything we can to prevent the use of forced labour.

Well, there were words there, but there was no guarantee, so let me just remind the House that the Secretary of State has sold his entire agenda as being one of providing moral leadership to the rest of the world, but there is no moral leadership in sending British children to schools powered by Chinese slaves.
An estimated 68% of UK panels come from China, and two thirds of NHS trusts are dependent on Chinese solar.

highlights.summary: The Home Secretary, Shabana Mahmood, ruled out launching a formal review of the religious exemption that allows Sikhs to carry the kirpan, saying she would speak first to the Sikh community, knife crime campaigners and the police.
highlights.key_quote: I would want to start that process by discussing the matter with the Sikh community themselves, knife crime campaigners and the police. I think that is the right way to proceed, rather than launching a formal review.
highlights.analysis: Mahmood was pressed by Liberal Democrat MP Cameron Thomas to review the exemption in light of the Nowak murder, and earlier in the statement by Paul Holmes (CON). She declined a formal review, drawing a line between religious observance and the criminal use of a knife, and pointed to the Offensive Weapons Act 2019 protections she had cited in her opening statement. The judge in the case had found that this was not an act of religious observance.

debate_map.quote: I believe this murder may give reasonable grounds to review existing exemptions to knife legislation on the grounds of religious reasons and national dress.
Could she also outline whether she is currently looking at whether she needs to make changes to the exemptions to carrying knives for religious or cultural purposes?
The exemption for the carrying of knives for religious and ceremonial purposes has been a long-standing arrangement, as a way of balancing public safety and religious freedom.

highlights.summary: Gurinder Singh Josan (LAB), a practising Nanaksari Sikh who carries the kirpan as part of his daily religious observance, condemned the murder as having no religious justification and asked to meet the Home Secretary to discuss safety, knife crime and the freedom to practise faith.
highlights.key_quote: As a Nanaksari Sikh, I wear the five Ks every day as part of my religious observance, including the kirpan, and as a Sikh, I really struggle to express the feelings of sheer horror that this case has generated within me, my family and my friends.
highlights.analysis: Josan spoke from inside the Sikh tradition to which the convicted murderer also belonged, telling the House that Digwa's actions are universally condemned across the community. The Home Secretary, herself a member of a faith minority, agreed to meet and said the balance between religious freedoms and public protection would need careful handling. Mr Tanmanjeet Singh Dhesi (LAB) made a parallel intervention earlier in the statement about Sikh service in both world wars.

debate_map.quote: I am always happy to discuss with my hon. Friend the different issues raised in this case, and in particular how we get the right balance between the religious freedoms he and I both enjoy as members of faith minorities in our country and the need to make sure that public protection is never compromised.

It is very galling that the likes of Reform, Restore and the far right decided to politicise people’s pain, attacking the Sikh community for wearing the kirpan and wanting it banned, even though the kirpan was not used in this violent attack.

highlights.summary: The shadow Armed Forces Minister, Mr Mark Francois (CON), told the Commons that the Defence Investment Plan promised at the strategic defence review is held up because the Chancellor refuses to sign it, and that the delay is now being criticised by the chairman of the NATO Military Committee.
highlights.key_quote: The problem is that the Chancellor of the Exchequer adamantly refuses to sign it, because if she signed it, she would have to say how she is going to pay for it.
highlights.analysis: Francois said the staff work and costings were completed months ago and that £2.6 billion of in-year operational cuts to defence last year and £3.5 billion this year have run alongside the delay. The Minister for the Armed Forces, Al Carns, replied that the plan will be published before the NATO summit on 7 to 8 July and confirmed in response to a follow-up question from Lincoln Jopp (CON) that he has now seen it.

debate_map.quote: Has my right hon. Friend had a chance to look at the report published by techUK, which represents a lot of small and medium-sized companies in the defence tech sector, and seen what it has to say, objectively, about the number of jobs that are being lost in the sector, the lack of investment in the sector, the pressure that its members are coming under and the sector’s lack of viability given this continued, unbearable delay?
Have the Minister and the Veterans Minister now seen the defence investment plan?
Yes, I have seen the defence investment plan.

highlights.summary: Jen Craft (LAB), Henry Nowak's constituency MP for Thurrock, used a point of order to ask whether Robert Jenrick (REF) owed an apology for setting out details of the case during business questions on 21 May, while the criminal trial against Nowak's murderer was still ongoing.
highlights.key_quote: I am led to believe that matters subject to ongoing criminal cases are considered to be sub judice and that Members should not speak about the details of such cases due to the risk of prejudicing proceedings and, in the worst-case scenario, causing a mistrial.
highlights.analysis: Craft framed the matter as a sub judice breach by a senior Member of Parliament and reminded the House that her constituents had refrained from voicing their views publicly until the trial concluded. The Deputy Speaker, Ms Nusrat Ghani, did not adjudicate Jenrick's conduct but reasserted that the sub judice resolution is a self-imposed restriction on freedom of speech and that the responsibility to avoid breaching it sits with each Member.

debate_map.quote: It is, however, ultimately the Member’s responsibility to avoid infringing the sub judice rule, and to exercise freedom of speech responsibly.
I pay tribute to my constituent, Henry Nowak, a bright young man at the start of a life that held so much promise.

highlights.summary: Ben Obese-Jecty (CON), a former Army officer, told the House that Commonwealth service personnel face costs near £10,000 to bring a spouse and two children to indefinite leave to remain, and accused successive Governments of stalling a manifesto pledge to waive those fees for veterans' dependants.
highlights.key_quote: A service member, their spouse and two children now potentially face a cost of just shy of £10,000 for the right to live in the country they have risked their life to defend.
highlights.analysis: Obese-Jecty moved new clause 5, which would amend the Immigration Act 2014 to waive indefinite leave to remain fees for the spouses and dependants of serving or previously serving armed forces personnel. The Minister for the Armed Forces, Al Carns, said the Government remained committed to the manifesto pledge but that the new clause as drafted was too broad and would cut across the existing statutory framework. Mr Calvin Bailey (LAB), a member of the all-party group on the armed forces community and a former service member, agreed with the underlying intent but said the Home Office must lead under the covenant duty rather than through the Bill.

debate_map.quote: the armed forces covenant places a legal responsibility on all Departments to remove those barriers and impediments to service life
The very least we can do is help them and their dependants by scrapping visa fees after four years of service.

highlights.summary: Sir Julian Lewis (CON) drew the Minister's attention to the Milburn report's finding that the surge in young people not in education, employment or training is being driven by anxiety and depression rather than serious mental illness, and asked for further research into why so many feel they cannot cope.
highlights.key_quote: It is mental health conditions that are now the most commonly reported health condition among NEET young people…This explosion has primarily been in mental health issues such as anxiety and depression, rather than in serious mental illnesses
highlights.analysis: Lewis cited paragraphs 424 and 425 of the report and offered his own diagnosis of 'a loss of mental resilience' among young people. The Minister, Andrew Western, agreed and acknowledged that 40 per cent of NEETs now cite mental health as a driver, a figure that has almost doubled in recent years. Rachael Maskell (LAB) had earlier asked for cross-departmental work with health on early intervention.

debate_map.quote: I agree that 40% of young people citing mental health conditions as a driver for their not being in employment, education or training is a concerning increase—it has almost doubled in recent years.
I particularly want to focus on mental health, and the fact that we do not have the right support in place for young people much earlier than the point at which they seek employment.

highlights.summary: Jim McMahon (LAB/Co-op) said apprenticeship starts have fallen 35 per cent since the levy was introduced and 68 per cent at level 2, and asked why public sector employers cannot be required to advertise apprenticeships at the point pupils leave school.
highlights.key_quote: Since the apprenticeship levy was brought in, the number of starts has dropped by 35% and the number of level 2 starts has dropped by 68%.
highlights.analysis: McMahon argued that part of the rise in young people becoming NEETs reflects 'the gap that young people fall through' between exam season and the autumn term, when only 16 per cent of apprenticeships are advertised. The Minister, Andrew Western, did not commit to a public-sector advertising rule but pointed to the Prime Minister's revised target of two thirds of young people in either apprenticeship or higher education, replacing the longstanding 50 per cent university target.

debate_map.quote: the Prime Minister’s revised target, not of 50% of young people going to university, but of having two thirds of young people in either an apprenticeship or higher education.
if we are to fix this crisis of youth unemployment, we need not tweaks, but an overhaul of vocational training and a correction to the Blairite obsession with university

highlights.summary: The shadow Energy Secretary, Claire Coutinho (CON), asked who is legally accountable if a nationwide blackout occurred from grid instability, citing the near-breach of the electricity grid's frequency limit on 2 May.
highlights.key_quote: On 2 May, our electricity grid almost breached its frequency limit. That has the potential to cause nationwide blackouts.
highlights.analysis: Coutinho linked the near-miss to the rising share of intermittent renewables in the system and said the Government's plans were making the grid harder to balance. The Energy Secretary, Ed Miliband, did not address the accountability question, calling the line 'the worst sort of scaremongering' and pointing to a CBI Economics report published the same day showing one million UK jobs supported by net zero.

debate_map.quote: I am afraid that the shadow Secretary of State is indulging in the worst sort of scaremongering to justify her anti-clean-energy agenda.

highlights.summary: Josh Babarinde (LD) told the House that his late constituent Pauline Cole, a former staff sergeant, lost most of her pension credit when she was awarded a war disablement pension because the Department for Work and Pensions treats military compensation as income for pension credit calculations.
highlights.key_quote: Before receiving that compensation, Pauline had been in receipt of £76.96 a week in pension credit. After receiving that compensation, her pension credit collapsed to just £9.23 a week.
highlights.analysis: Babarinde said military compensation is disregarded for universal credit but counted against pension credit, a quirk the Royal British Legion calls a breach of the armed forces covenant. He told the House the Department has said it has 'no plans to change this policy' and has not assessed the cost of fixing it, and asked the Minister to undertake a financial assessment and meet Pauline's sons before Report stage. The Minister for the Armed Forces, Al Carns, said in his closing reply that the Government would take the argument forward.

debate_map.quote: we will continue to take his argument forward in Government.

highlights.debate: Relationship between Social Security Scotland and the DWP
highlights.summary: Wendy Chamberlain (LD) told the Commons that the Department for Work and Pensions has been wrongly deducting the Scottish carer support payment from her constituents' universal credit even where they had only an underlying entitlement, and warned that more than 30,000 carers in Scotland could be affected.
highlights.key_quote: there are more than 30,000 carers across Scotland who have this underlying entitlement and could be missing out on universal credit as a result.
highlights.analysis: Chamberlain set out two specific cases, one constituent at state pension age and one on employment and support allowance, where the Department continued to deduct a Scottish payment that was never received. The Minister, Andrew Western, who is responsible for the Department's relationship with Social Security Scotland, acknowledged the issue, noted that equivalent problems in England and Wales had been identified by the Sayce review of carer's allowance overpayments, and committed to take the case-specific issues away.

debate_map.quote: if she wants to escalate specific cases to me, I am very happy to have a look at them.
I have heard the specifics of what the hon. Member has said for the first time today, and I will take that issue away.

sections.summary: The House met at half-past Eleven o'clock, prayers were said, and Mr Speaker took the Chair to open the sitting on Tuesday 2 June 2026.

Oral questions to the Secretary of State for Energy Security and Net Zero, Ed Miliband, and his Ministers, against the backdrop of the Ofgem energy price cap rising 13 per cent from July following the war in Iran. Questions covered the impact on bills, including a £150 reduction in costs the Government has put in place and an expanded warm home discount; the just transition for North Sea oil and gas workers, with Conservative and Reform members pressing for new drilling licences; deep geothermal energy; clean power and small modular reactors; household and business energy bills; the EU internal electricity market; the use of Chinese-manufactured solar panels and forced labour in supply chains; heat networks; voluntary carbon markets; and topical questions covering grid stability after the 2 May frequency near-breach, red diesel costs, planning applications for solar farms and an AQUIND interconnector. The shadow Secretary of State, Claire Coutinho, and the Member for Reform Fareham and Waterlooville, Suella Braverman, offered condolences to the Secretary of State on the recent death of his mother.

Urgent question granted to the shadow Work and Pensions Secretary, Helen Whately (CON), on the publication of Alan Milburn's interim report on young people not in education, employment or training, with the Parliamentary Under-Secretary, Andrew Western, replying. The report finds that the NEET problem is long-standing and deep-seated, with mental health (chiefly anxiety and depression) now the most commonly reported health condition among NEETs and accounting for 40 per cent of cases. Conservative members linked the rise to recent changes to national insurance, business rates and the Employment Rights Act, which the Minister rebutted by quoting from paragraphs 266 and 268 of the report. Members across the House raised work experience, further education funding, devolved delivery, supported internships for young people with education, health and care plans, the role of family hubs and Sure Start, support for young carers, coastal and place-based deprivation, and the impact of artificial intelligence on entry-level jobs. The Minister set out the youth guarantee, financial incentives for hiring young people and apprentices, and the Prime Minister's revised target of two thirds of young people in apprenticeship or higher education. Full recommendations from Alan Milburn are expected later in the year.

Statement by the Home Secretary, Shabana Mahmood, on the murder of 18-year-old Henry Nowak, the day after Vickrum Digwa was sentenced to life imprisonment with a minimum term of 21 years, with Digwa's mother Kiran Kaur convicted of assisting an offender and the Crown Prosecution Service authorising further charges against other family members. Police body-worn camera footage released the previous night shows Henry handcuffed and disbelieved as he lay dying. The Home Secretary committed to a resourced Independent Office for Police Conduct investigation, expected to report within three months, and ruled out launching a formal review of the religious exemption that allows Sikhs to carry the kirpan, saying she would speak first to the Sikh community, knife crime campaigners and the police. She set the response in the context of the Government's plan to halve knife crime over a decade, citing a 27 per cent fall in knife homicides to a decade low. The shadow Home Secretary, Chris Philp (CON), called for the College of Policing's anti-racism commitment to be withdrawn; Robert Jenrick and Suella Braverman, both Reform members, framed the case as evidence of an anti-racism agenda and asked the Home Secretary to affirm 'white lives matter', which she declined to do in those terms. Gurinder Singh Josan (LAB), a Nanaksari Sikh, and Mr Tanmanjeet Singh Dhesi (LAB) spoke as members of the Sikh community condemning the murder. Members across the House condemned online misinformation, including the misidentification of an unrelated officer who was forced to relocate after receiving death threats.

Jen Craft (LAB), Henry Nowak's constituency MP, raised a point of order asking whether Robert Jenrick (REF) owed an apology for setting out details of the Nowak case during business questions on 21 May, while the criminal trial was still ongoing. The Deputy Speaker, Ms Nusrat Ghani, did not adjudicate the individual case but reasserted that the sub judice resolution is a self-imposed restriction on freedom of speech in the House and that responsibility for compliance rests with each Member. Carla Lockhart (DUP) raised a further point of order asking whether the Southport girls and Sir David Amess cases had been debated in the House before sentencing; the Deputy Speaker said the Chair is not responsible for the content of speeches and that the Government decides which statements to bring forward. A short programme motion on the Armed Forces Bill was put and agreed.

Committee of the whole House on the Armed Forces Bill, taken on the Floor over two days. The Minister for the Armed Forces, Al Carns, set out Government amendments extending the armed forces covenant duty to the Greater London Authority and combined authorities; broadening compulsory purchase powers for a new Defence Housing Service backed by £9 billion over a decade and expected to modernise 40,000 homes; reforming service justice with new service stalking and restraining orders, image deletion orders, and clearer victim choice between civilian and service jurisdictions; and simplifying reserve recall liability to a single age of 65 to align with NATO partners. Opposition amendments addressed the portability of education, health and care plans and adoption arrangements for service families (Mr Mark Francois, CON), waiving indefinite leave to remain fees for the spouses and dependants of Commonwealth service personnel (Ben Obese-Jecty, CON), extending Renters' Rights Act standards to single living accommodation (James MacCleary, LD), and the prompt referral of sexual offences and domestic abuse to civilian police (also LD). Mr Francois accused the Chancellor of refusing to sign the Defence Investment Plan; the Minister confirmed in reply that he has seen the plan and it will be published before the NATO summit on 7 to 8 July. Several new clauses were pressed to votes and divisions deferred to the following day; the Member who tabled new clause 5 was absent at the moment of decision, and Mr Francois moved it formally on his behalf.

Procedural items only: the regulations applying renters' rights protections to military single living accommodation, and the Agriculture (Delinked Payments) (Reductions) (England) Regulations 2026, were both subject to challenges and divisions deferred to the following day under Standing Order No. 41A. No substantive debate.

Liam Conlon (LAB) presented a petition signed by nearly 10,000 residents urging the Government to encourage Bromley Council to maintain the borough's membership of the Greater London Authority, citing the older person's freedom pass, Zip travel passes for young people, free school meals in primary schools and the Crystal Palace National Sports Centre as benefits residents would lose. The petition was prompted by Reform UK's stated intention to take Bromley out of London.

Adjournment debate secured by Wendy Chamberlain (LD) on the relationship between the Department for Work and Pensions and Social Security Scotland. Chamberlain set out two constituent cases of repeated wrongful deductions from universal credit because the Department had treated her constituents as receiving the Scottish carer support payment when they had only an underlying entitlement, and warned that more than 30,000 carers in Scotland may be affected. Jim Shannon (DUP) said equivalent communication failures occur in Northern Ireland. The Minister, Andrew Western, who is the Government's representative on the joint ministerial working group on welfare, said he had only heard the specifics for the first time and would take them away; work to automatically offset such benefits will begin in the next financial year and is intended to be completed within this Parliament. He confirmed the issue was also identified for England and Wales by the Sayce review of carer's allowance overpayments.
